literal: heroic / staunch devotee of Śiva; from vīra (hero) + śaiva (devotee of Śiva)
invariant_definition: Vīraśaiva (also Liṅgāyata) is a devotional Śaiva tradition that crystallized in 12th-century Karnataka around Basava and the vacana poet-saints (śaraṇas), with later Sanskrit systematization as Śakti-viśiṣṭādvaita. Its members worship Śiva alone and wear a personal liṅga (iṣṭa-liṅga) as the constant locus of devotion. Philosophically it teaches a qualified non-dualism in which the soul, by stages of devotion and discipline (the ṣaṭ-sthala, "six stages"), progresses to union with Śiva. Socially the movement was marked by its rejection of caste hierarchy, Brahminical ritualism, and temple-centered priestcraft in favor of direct, egalitarian devotion expressed in vernacular Kannada vacanas.
